channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Theos: Cities of Myth Is A "Spiritual Successor To Classic City-Builders"
item.content:encoded:
Today, Dotemu and Triskell Interactive have announced Theos: Cities of Myth. A follow-up to 2023's Pharaoh: A New Era, this new city builder brings players to Ancient Greece, where they can create a city in honor of the Greek Pantheon, build up trade relations with different ports and cities, and engage in historical and mythological strategy.

Ahead of this announcement, we had the chance to hear about the game from Theophile Noire, Creative Director at Triskall Interactive, and get hands-on with the tutorial and first mission of the game.

To give a top-level overview of what to expect, Noire explained that Theos: Cities of Myth has been built as a "spiritual successor to classic city builders". You'll dedicate your city to one of seven Olympian Gods, build up your cities, learn more about them historically as well as mythologically, and engage in combat and trade.

The game will be comprised of scenarios to complete and progress through the game. The early scenario that we got to play simply consisted of building your city up from the ground and reaching certain developmental milestones like building houses of a certain quality, establishing trade, and then upgrading our Sanctuary to Athena.

Playing through the tutorials, I had a good time building up my foundational knowledge of progression. Theos does a good job walking you through creating the basics and also shows you how parts of your town will upgrade automatically, while others need to be done manually.

Placing a house down will immediately have new villagers flock to it; that's how you get a level 1 house. From there, by introducing a fountain and setting a walking path for the water carriers, each house will automatically upgrade to level 2, increasing your prosperity while also allowing more people to move in. Level 3 requires food, and Level 4 requires culture.

How To Best Supply Your City

The walking paths can be set automatically, letting an efficient path be drawn around your whole village. I did find in this build that the logic was sometimes a bit behind. After adding to my city, if I immediately tried to have the walking path redrawn, it would sometimes ignore the new road and the houses on it. After a few minutes, I could press it again, and it would figure it out. This automatic system is good for the early game, but you'll very quickly stop relying on it as your city grows and you have two or three fountains that you'll be wanting to service different parts of the city.

I was a big fan of being able to place a house and then be able to visually watch the city grow as those buildings develop on their own. There's no point in the game, you'll have to pause making progress and growing to re-zone areas with Level 1 houses, demolishing and building new, more expensive houses, because they'll upgrade with you.

You'll also see the streets slowly paved over as areas become more and more developed. This works as a natural visual indicator that you might need to put more work into a certain part of town if the streets aren't white, but also creates a natural transition from paved streets to dirt roads as you might transition from the city center to farmlands around the edge. These choices are eye-catching as well as functional.

Moving out of the tutorial, where more of the game was still in progress, some difficulties began to arise. The first complication I had was with prompts, or really the lack of clear prompts. After getting through the levels of progress I was familiar with, we got to establishing trading posts, creating quarries, and workshops for refining marble into blocks.

Prompts Need Some Work, Plenty Of Time

The game prompted that the next step in the scenario would be to create these things, so I did, but having never seen them before, where to best place them, what they needed to run, and how much space they needed was all new. There was also a lot of temporary UI in these scenarios, plain text and quantity indicators that will likely be easier to review and understand once the game is ready for public hands.

Some buildings that did give indicators, such as circles above the structure showing a warehouse was out of space, unfortunately blended in with the other colorful buildings around it. I was sitting and waiting for a Marble Block to be added to my sanctuary for a few minutes before scouring through the production line and discovering that the lack of space in a Warehouse was holding up the entire process.

A lot of the above problems will be resolved with finished assets and familiarity with the game. Things tripping you up in Scenario 1 are likely not going to be an issue at all by Scenario 5, 10, or 20. 

My small dip into Theos: Cities of Myth had a lot of promise. As someone not too familiar with the genre, I enjoyed how forgiving it was to the initial placement of houses and required supporting buildings like the Fountain and Fire House. I also really enjoyed that the city continued to visually develop as you added things like living resources and then culture. I'd be very excited to see how pretty and complicated cities look as you continue through the game.

With so much going on, there's a fair bit to be done to help ease the players into the full experience, but there's plenty of time still to add the polish to what's already a very functional foundation of a game.

Theos: Cities of Myth was previewed on PC via Steam with an early build provided by the publisher over the course of 2 hours - Photos were provided by the publisher

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/theos-cities-of-myth-is-spiritual-successor-to-classic-city-builders
